sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 104, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: 102 actions. SPACE consumed 8px of yellow L-shape (now 37px total in fragments). Character at rows 39-47, cols 48-56. Need to continue consuming L-shape. Let me press SPACE again.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 103 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check if the SPACE action consumed more of the yellow L-shape.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: 103 actions. Pressed SPACE again. Let me check if more of the yellow L-shape was consumed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state - focus on yellow pixels
seg = current_frame.segmentation
yellow_total = 0
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        print(f"Y: id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}")
        yellow_total += n['pixels']
print(f"Total yellow: {yellow_total}")

# Check character position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 40:
        print(f"Character: boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current state:
- Yellow L-shape fragments: 27 + 9 + 1 = 37px (same as before)
- Yellow border bar: 32px (was 33px, so decreased by 1)
- Total yellow: 69px (37 + 32)
So the SPACE action didn't consume more of the L-shape. The yellow border bar decreased by 1px (from 33 to 32), which is probably just the natural fuel consumption from the step.
The character is still at rows 39-47, cols 48-56.
The yellow L-shape fragments are:
- id=11: 27px at cols 51-53, rows 45-53 (left vertical bar)
- id=12: 9px at cols 57-59, rows 45-47 (right horizontal bar)
- id=13: 1px at (46, 55)
The character overlaps with these fragments. Maybe I need to move to a different position to consume more of the L-shape.
Actually, looking at the first time I consumed the yellow L-shape (when I moved through it earlier), it went from 45px to 29px (16px consumed). Then pressing SPACE consumed 8px more (from 29 to 21... wait, let me recalculate).
Actually, earlier the yellow L-shape was at 45px, then after moving through it it was at 29px. Then after pressing SPACE once, it was at 37px (27 + 9 + 1). That doesn't make sense - it should have decreased, not increased.
Wait, let me re-examine. Earlier, the yellow L-shape was at 45px. After moving through it, it was at 29px. But that was a different instance of the L-shape (before the game reset).
After the game reset, the yellow L-shape was at 45px again. Then I moved DOWN and pressed SPACE, and it fragmented into 27 + 9 + 1 = 37px. So 8px were consumed.
Now after pressing SPACE again, the L-shape is still at 37px. So the second SPACE didn't consume anything.
Maybe I need to move to a different position to consume more of the L-shape. Or maybe the SPACE action only works once per L-shape.
Let me try moving RIGHT to overlap with more of the L-shape and then pressing SPACE again.
Actually, looking at the character position (cols 48-56) and the L-shape fragments:
- id=11: cols 51-53 (overlaps with character)
- id=12: cols 57-59 (just to the right of character)
- id=13: col 55 (overlaps with character)
The character overlaps with ids 11 and 13, but not with id 12. Maybe I need to move RIGHT to overlap with id 12 and then press SPACE.
Let me try moving RIGHT 1 step and then pressing SPACE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: 103 actions. SPACE didn't consume more L-shape (still 37px). Character overlaps with some fragments but not all. Let me move RIGHT to overlap with more fragments and press SPACE again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move RIGHT to overlap with more L-shape fragments, then press SPACE
action(['RIGHT', 'SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
